one_line_takeaway: AgentDyn is a benchmark-methodology paper showing that dynamic-planning, helpful-third-party-instructions, and multi-app task complexity expose severe over-defense and residual insecurity in prompt-injection defenses that look near-perfect on static benchmarks like AgentDojo (e.g., Meta SecAlign's ASR jumps from 1.9% on AgentDojo to 9.0% on AgentDyn).
key_finding: Defenses that appear near-perfect on existing benchmarks (some achieving near-zero ASR with minimal utility loss on AgentDojo) fail to reach acceptable security-utility tradeoffs on AgentDyn. From Table 3 (GPT-4o base agent, no-defense baseline: 53.33% benign utility, 55.52% utility-under-attack, 37.80% ASR): prompting-based defenses (Prompt Sandwiching, Spotlighting) preserve utility but barely reduce ASR (Spotlighting still 27.61% ASR on GPT-4o), showing insufficient security. Filtering-based defenses show severe over-defense: ProtectAI Detector collapses utility to 0.00%/0.56% (no-attack/under-attack) on GPT-4o while cutting ASR to 0.85%; PIGuard similarly collapses to 10.00%/1.46% utility; PromptGuard2 keeps 60.00% no-attack utility but drops to 20.80% under attack while still allowing 27.15% ASR, because it discards entire tool outputs (including needed information) whenever it flags an injection. Alignment-based Meta SecAlign-70B is the most balanced defense tested: 55.00% no-attack / 53.35% under-attack utility with 8.98% ASR, but the paper's Figure 2 comparison shows this same defense achieves only 1.9% ASR on AgentDojo versus 9.0% ASR on AgentDyn (over a fourfold increase) and 79.5% utility-under-attack on AgentDojo versus 53.4% on AgentDyn — directly demonstrating benchmark-driven overestimation of deployability. System-level defenses vary sharply: CaMeL achieves 0.00% ASR across all four agents but also 0.00% utility across all four agents on AgentDyn (its static-code-generation approach cannot handle open-ended, dynamically-replanned tasks at all). Tool Filter drops to 8.33%/4.91% utility on GPT-4o (over-defense from blocking tools that only become necessary after later interaction). Progent (dynamic policy updates) drops to 6.67%/5.83% utility on GPT-4o because it struggles to assign correct tool-access privileges as toolset size grows, with errors compounding over long trajectories. DRIFT retains more utility than CaMeL/Tool Filter/Progent (30.00%/27.09% on GPT-4o) but still degrades substantially versus its AgentDojo performance because it depends on an initial upfront plan. The trajectory-length analysis (Figure 5, vanilla GPT-4o) shows utility falling monotonically from 100% at trajectory length 2 to 23.6% at length 10+, while ASR follows a roughly unimodal curve peaking around trajectory length 6 (62.5% ASR), suggesting a 'sweet spot' where task complexity most favors the attacker. The authors also cite a literature survey finding that 79% of community effort in this space focuses on defenses, despite the paper's finding that essentially none of the tested defenses are readily deployable.
core_contribution: The paper introduces AgentDyn, a manually designed, open-ended benchmark for evaluating whether indirect-prompt-injection defenses for LLM agents are actually deployable in realistic, dynamic environments, rather than just secure on existing static benchmarks. The authors argue that prevalent agent-security benchmarks (InjecAgent, ASB, AgentDojo) have three structural flaws that let defenses 'game' the evaluation: (i) user tasks are static and fully plannable upfront, letting defenses appear secure merely by rigidly enforcing the initial plan (over-defense masquerading as security); (ii) third-party environment content contains almost no benign/helpful instructions, so defenses can score well simply by blindly discarding all external instructions; and (iii) tasks are short (1-3 steps), single-application, and small-tool-set, unlike real multi-app workflows. AgentDyn addresses these by manually constructing 60 open-ended user tasks and 28 injection tasks across three suites (Shopping, GitHub, Daily Life) covering 7 application scenarios (Shopping, GitHub, Email, Bank, Web, FileSystem, Calendar), crossed to produce 560 security test cases. Tasks require dynamic replanning based on environment feedback, embed at least one genuinely helpful third-party instruction on the critical execution path (e.g., an OTP-verification email required to complete checkout), and average 7.10 execution steps and 3.17 application scenarios per task (vs. AgentDojo's 3.49 steps / 1.38 apps, per the paper's own Table 1). The paper also introduces realistic design policies: HTML-formatted environment content instead of plain text, injections placed only in plausible attacker-controlled surfaces (e.g., an ad banner) rather than a benign user's own text, use of 'common knowledge' trust signals (e.g., official domains), and real-time interactable entities (e.g., an OTP email that actually arrives in the inbox mid-task). Using this benchmark, the authors evaluate 12 LLM agents (8 commercial: Gemini-2.5-Pro/Flash, Claude-Sonnet-3.5/4.5, GPT-4o/4o-mini/5-mini/5.1; 4 open-source: Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen3-235B, Qwen3-Coder, Kimi-K2.5) and 10 widely used defenses spanning prompting-based (Prompt Sandwiching, Spotlighting), filtering-based (ProtectAI Detector, PIGuard, PromptGuard2), alignment-based (Meta SecAlign-70B), and system-level (Tool Filter, CaMeL, Progent, DRIFT) categories, using the 'important_instructions' attack from AgentDojo as the default attack (plus ignore/completion/adaptive-attack variants in an appendix study on GPT-5-mini, GPT-5.1, and Claude-Sonnet-4.5).
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against tool-using LLM agents: a third party embeds malicious natural-language instructions inside external content the agent will retrieve during task execution (e.g., a webpage, email, or file), aiming to hijack the agent into performing an unintended, attacker-chosen action instead of (or alongside) the user's original task. The paper explicitly constrains the threat model to be realistic rather than maximally powerful: injected content may only appear in plausible attacker-controlled surfaces (e.g., a webpage's advertisement banner sold to third parties), not inside content authored by a benign party (e.g., not inside a personal website owner's own body text, which is how AgentDojo places injections). Injection instructions are also designed to be generalizable/non-targeted (no victim-specific personal data used to make the injection easier), reflecting a broad-audience attacker rather than a spear-phishing-style adversary. The default attack technique studied is the 'important_instructions' template from AgentDojo (framing the injected text with urgency/importance markers to get it prioritized over the user's actual request); the appendix additionally tests ignore-previous-instruction attacks, completion attacks, and manually crafted adaptive attacks.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations appendix states: (1) despite deliberate realism efforts, AgentDyn still cannot fully capture the complexity and diversity of real-world environments; (2) because attack research lags behind defense research in this field, the attacks included are relatively weak against the latest frontier models, and the paper's stated primary goal is evaluating utility/deployability rather than pushing attack strength; (3) the authors position stronger, more realistic attack framework development as complementary future work, and note the benchmark can be dynamically extended with future attacks. Additional caveats evident from the text itself: the benchmark is manually curated at a relatively small scale (60 user tasks, 28 injection tasks, 3 suites/7 application scenarios) and the authors themselves describe it in the discussion as 'just a small open-ended benchmark with limited scenarios and task complexity, which is still far from the real-world settings.' The main Table 3 results are reported only for 4 of the 12 evaluated agents in the body of the paper (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Claude-Sonnet-3.5, Llama-3.3-70B), with the authors explicitly choosing to exclude the newest models from the primary analysis on the hypothesis that they may have memorized the specific 'important_instructions' attack template, which is a reasonable methodological choice but means the headline numbers are not from the most current frontier models by default.

datasets_used: AgentDyn (introduced in this paper): 60 manually designed open-ended user tasks and 28 injection tasks across 3 suites (Shopping, GitHub, Dailylife) spanning 7 application scenarios, crossed into 560 security test cases
AgentDojo (used as a comparison benchmark, not introduced here)
InjecAgent and ASB (referenced only for benchmark-statistics comparison in Table 1, not used for the paper's own experiments)
quantitative_results: No-defense GPT-4o baseline on AgentDyn: 53.33% benign utility, 55.52% utility-under-attack, 37.80% ASR
Meta SecAlign-70B on AgentDyn: 55.00% benign utility, 53.35% utility-under-attack, 8.98% ASR
Meta SecAlign ASR: 1.9% on AgentDojo vs. 9.0% on AgentDyn (>4x increase), per Figure 2
Meta SecAlign utility-under-attack: 79.5% on AgentDojo vs. 53.4% on AgentDyn, per Figure 2
CaMeL on AgentDyn: 0.00% benign utility and 0.00% ASR across all four base agents (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Claude-Sonnet-3.5, Llama-3.3-70B)
ProtectAI Detector on GPT-4o (AgentDyn): 0.00% benign utility, 0.56% utility-under-attack, 0.85% ASR
PromptGuard2 on GPT-4o (AgentDyn): 60.00% benign utility, 20.80% utility-under-attack, 27.15% ASR
Tool Filter on GPT-4o (AgentDyn): 8.33% benign utility, 4.91% utility-under-attack, 4.22% ASR
Progent on GPT-4o (AgentDyn): 6.67% benign utility, 5.83% utility-under-attack, 1.69% ASR
DRIFT on GPT-4o (AgentDyn): 30.00% benign utility, 27.09% utility-under-attack, 0.83% ASR
Benchmark task-complexity comparison (Table 1): AgentDojo averages 19.87 tools/3.49 trajectory steps/1.38 apps per task vs. AgentDyn's 33.33 tools/7.10 trajectory steps/3.17 apps per task
Trajectory-length analysis on vanilla GPT-4o (Figure 5): utility falls from 100% (length 2) to 23.6% (length 10+); ASR peaks at 62.5% around trajectory length 6
Literature survey finding: 79% of surveyed community effort in prompt-injection defense research focuses on defenses
